text: get dates values examples manually iDAI.chronontology. Let’s use Roman Republic Roman Empire easy example. Roman Republic contains three basic periods: “Early Roman Republic”, “High Roman Republic”, “Late Roman Republic”. Roman Empire also contains three periods: “Early Roman Empire”, “High Roman Empire” “Late Roman Empire”). Thus, data.frame (spreadsheet) need least six rows. code: derive_period(data, conc, min, max, previous_start, previous_end)

title: Derive Period from Absolute Dates — derive_period
text: data data.frame containing least two columns minimum maximum absolute dating row integer. values need correspond possible dating ranges present concordance used (see make_chrongler_conc()). conc chrongler.conc, required, built make_chrongler_conc(). min chr/int. Name index column minimum dating (integers representing years). max chr/int. Name index column maximum dating (integers representing years). previous_start chr/int, optional. Name index pre-existing column containing ending period. supplied, previously empty values (NA) populated new period.end column. comment stored column called period.source.

what: Produce a Concordance for Wrangling Categorical Data with chrongler — make_chrongler_conc
title: Produce a Concordance for Wrangling Categorical Data with chrongler — make_chrongler_conc
text: chrongler assumes periods come groups: set larger scale periods "era"s, can span multiple periods. vignette demonstrates example Roman Republic Roman Empire, encompassing multiple "sub-periods". Time periods also beginning end can expressed years BCE/CE. chrongler currently uses negative positive numeric values dates. (aware potential year-0-errors processing data.) make_chrongler_conc() can produce concordance periods groups, start, end dates, used chrongler-functions reformat data.

title: Produce a Concordance for Wrangling Categorical Data with chrongler — make_chrongler_conc
code: make_chrongler_conc(   file,   cols = list(group = NA, values = NA, dating.min = NA, dating.max = NA, color = NA,     source = NA),   ... )

title: Produce a Concordance for Wrangling Categorical Data with chrongler — make_chrongler_conc
text: file chr / data.frame: Path csv-file read read.csv(), data.frame. cols named list mapping expected column roles corresponding column names (chr) indices (int) file. following names recognised: group : column containing period group names values : column containing period names, chronological order dating.min : column containing earliest absolute date respective period (negative = BCE, positive = CE) dating.max : column containing latest absolute date respective period (negative = BCE, positive = CE) color (optional) : column containing colour value per period source (optional) : column containing source reference per period column names already match names , can omit argument entirely detected automatically. ... arguments passed read.csv() file path.

title: Produce a Concordance for Wrangling Categorical Data with chrongler — make_chrongler_conc
text: named list class chrongler.conc following elements: — named list one entry per period (including group-level periods). entry contains: name — period name (chr) group — group period belongs (chr) dating — list absolute dates (num) color — associated colour value (chr), NULL supplied source — associated source reference, NULL supplied grouped — named list one entry per group. entry ordered factor periods belonging group, chronological order. group.order — ordered factor group names, chronological order. period.order — ordered factor non-group period names, chronological order. dating — named list one list(, ) entry per period, including group-level periods. color — named character vector colour values, one per period. source — named list source references, one per period. class used chrongler functions process data.

title: Produce a Concordance for Wrangling Categorical Data with chrongler — make_chrongler_conc
text: data.frame csv-file supply function ordered, .e. earlier periods come rows later periods. Warnings supplied dating seems order. happens value dating.max lower equal corresponding value dating.min.
